data.title: China folk custom beats women to boost fertility, with dire consequences if fail to conceive
data.description:
An uncivilised folk custom in ancient China known as Pai Xi, or “Beating for Joy”, involved striking women with sticks in an attempt to exorcise evil spirits and pray for conception.
Pai Xi, also known as Da Sheng, meaning “Beating for Birth”, is believed to have originated in southern China, particularly in Jiangsu and Fujian provinces.
It was a fertility ritual that gained popularity during the Song dynasty (960–1279).
According to the Records of National Customs of China, in rural areas of Taixing, Jiangsu province in southeastern China, on the Lantern Festival, the 15th day of the first lunar month, if a woman had not conceived within two years of marriage, she would be dragged out of her home and chased through the village.

Villagers would strike her with bamboo sticks, brooms or wooden clubs.
Her husband and in-laws would not intervene; instead, they viewed the beating ritual as a hopeful sign that good fortune and pregnancy were on its way.
As long as her face was not injured, any other part of her body could be beaten.
The ritual would end only when her husband felt it had gone on long enough.
At that point, he would bring out peanuts and red dates, which are symbols of fertility, and distribute them to the people who had beaten his wife.
Only then would the crowd disperse, often with a remark: “If she does not get pregnant by next year, we will chase and beat her again.”

Each village has slightly different customs when it comes to the “Beating the Bride” ritual.
In some variations found in Fujian province, villagers would beat the new bride while repeatedly asking: “Will you give birth?”
The bride is expected to respond: “Yes.”
If she acts shy or stubbornly refuses to answer, the beating continues until she gives in.
This ritual is repeated annually until the woman conceives.
In the eastern city of Taizhou’s Huangyan district, women who had been married for years without bearing children would go to the City God Temple to pray for a child.

They would bare their shoulders, kneel before the deity and be lightly whipped by female companions while chanting: “May the gods witness my sincerity, and grant me a mythical child.”
In some cases, an even more extreme ritual known as “burning lazy eyebrows” was practised, where women unable to conceive were swung back and forth over a fire.
This gross and uncivilised custom, which has largely disappeared in modern China, stemmed from feudal superstitions and the deeply rooted belief that the purpose of marriage was to “continue the family line”.
Under this ideology, women were expected, or even obliged, to bear children, preferably sons, turning them into “reproductive machines”.

data.title: Is Louvre ticket price increase for non-Europeans ‘unabashed nationalism’?
data.description:
Should foreign tourists pay more for state-funded galleries than locals, or should art be accessible to all without discrimination?
France is hiking prices for non-Europeans at the Louvre this week, provoking debate about so-called “dual pricing”.
From January 14, any adult visitor from outside the European Union, Iceland, Liechtenstein and Norway will have to pay €32 (US$37) to enter the Louvre – a 45 per cent increase – while the Palace of Versailles will up its prices by three euros.
Americans, UK citizens and Chinese nationals, who are some of the museum’s most numerous foreign visitors, will be among those affected, as will tourists from poorer countries.

The French move has few precedents elsewhere in Europe, but is more common in developing countries, where tariffs at sites such as Machu Picchu in Peru or the Taj Mahal in India vary.
Trade unions at the Louvre have denounced the policy as “shocking philosophically, socially and on a human level” and have called for strike action over the change, along with a raft of other complaints.
They argue that the museum’s vast collection of 500,000 items, including many from Egypt, the Middle East and Africa, hold universal human value.
While rejecting discriminatory pricing on principle, they are also worried for practical reasons, as staff will now need to check visitors’ identity papers.
French academic Patrick Poncet has drawn a parallel between France’s move and the policies of US President Donald Trump, whose administration hiked the cost of visiting US National Parks for foreign tourists by US$100 on January 1.
The French policy was “symptomatic of the return, as elsewhere in the world, of unabashed nationalism”, Poncet wrote in the newspaper Le Monde last month.
Other state-owned French tourist hotspots are hiking their fees for all visitors, including the Château de Chambord in the Loire region and the national opera house in Paris.
The government has justified the increases on financial grounds, looking to raise €20 million to €30 million annually at a time when it is under pressure to boost revenues and cut spending.
Some of the funds will go towards a colossal plan to renovate the Louvre, which French President Emmanuel Macron announced last year.
Estimated to cost around a billion euros, the project has been called wasteful by unions and some art critics.
Everyone agrees the Louvre is in poor shape, however, with a recent water leak, structural problems and an embarrassing daylight robbery in October focusing minds.
“I want visitors from outside the EU to pay more for their entry tickets and for that surcharge to go toward funding the renovation of our national heritage,” French Culture Minister Rachida Dati said at the end of 2024 as she announced the hikes.
“The French are not meant to pay for everything all by themselves,” she added.

It remains to be seen whether the break with European convention by the continent’s most-visited country will spur other cultural destinations to follow suit.
Pricing based on age is commonplace in Europe, with access for under-18s free at places such as the Acropolis in Athens, the Prado in Madrid and the Colosseum in Rome to encourage them to visit.
The Louvre will remain free for minors from all countries and Europeans under 26.
Other destinations, such as the Doge’s Palace in Venice, offer free entrance for city residents.
Britain has long had a policy of offering universal free access to permanent collections at its national galleries and museums.
But the former director of the British Museum, Mark Jones, backed fee-paying in one of his last interviews in charge, telling The Sunday Times newspaper in 2024 that “it would make sense for us to charge overseas visitors for admission”.
The proposal prompted debate but has not been adopted.
A research paper published last year by The Cultural Policy Unit, a British museum think tank, opposed it for both practical and philosophical reasons.
It would reduce entries, lengthen queue times and overturn a centuries-old policy, the report concluded.
“Britain holds its national collections for the world – not just its own residents,” it said.

data.title: US aircraft carrier steams from South China Sea to Mideast after Trump threat against Iran
data.description:
A US aircraft carrier has reportedly left the South China Sea after a live-fire drill and is heading to the Middle East amid anti-government protests in Iran.
US cable news network NewsNation reported on Thursday that the Pentagon ordered the USS Abraham Lincoln and its carrier strike group, which includes Arleigh Burke-class guided-missile destroyers, to leave their position in the South China Sea and sail towards the Middle East.
According to the US Naval Institute’s carrier tracker, there were no US aircraft carriers deployed in the Middle East under the US Central Command as of Monday. The Abraham Lincoln was the closest to the region of all the carrier strike groups on active duty, operating in the waters near Scarborough Shoal.
It left its homeport of San Diego in November and has been in the South China Sea since last month, operating for at least two weeks in the resource-rich waterway claimed by various countries in the region.




According to footage released by the Pentagon’s Defence Visual Information Distribution Service, the Abraham Lincoln conducted various training activities last week.
These included the live-fire drill on January 8, an exercise that involved a Phalanx close-in weapons system – an array of mounted turrets designed to counter incoming missiles, aircraft and boats. The drill also involved flight operations, replenishments at sea, damage control training and explosive ordnance disposal drills, according to the service.
It came just days before rival claimants China and the Philippines traded blame yet again for clashes in the waters.
On Monday, Manila rejected Beijing’s claims that the Philippines was taking provocative action in the South China Sea. It accused China of “persistent illegal, coercive, aggressive and deceptive” activities, an accusation that the Chinese embassy in Manila said on Tuesday was “unfounded and misleading”.
In mid-December, Chinese coastguard ships targeted Philippine fishing boats with water cannons near Sabina Shoal in the disputed Spratly Islands.
China said it took “necessary control measures” against the large number of vessels from the Philippines, while Manila said three fishermen were injured and two boats damaged in the incident.


The US aircraft carrier leaves the area for the Middle East where Iran is experiencing its biggest anti-government protests since the 1979 Iranian revolution, with reports of between 2,000 and 12,000 people killed in Tehran’s crackdown.
On Thursday, Tehran ordered a temporary closure of its airspace, which lasted for around five hours, after US President Donald Trump signalled a possible military strike against the Islamic regime if the crackdown continued.
Trump wrote on his social media on Tuesday that Iranian authorities would “pay a big price” for the killing of civilians and urged people in the country to “keep protesting”, adding that “help is on its way”.
But he appeared to temper his rhetoric on Wednesday, saying the “killing in Iran is stopping”.
Tehran warned that it would target US forces in the Middle East region if Washington launched an attack against the country, prompting a number of US and British military personnel to leave the Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar.

data.title: Softcovers and selfies: China’s bookshops find fame as photogenic backdrops
data.description: In China, bookstores are the new hotspot, but not because of what’s on the shelves. Eye-catching interior designs are drawing photographers and influencers to snap photos among the bookshelves.\nBut not everyone is happy about this new trend. China’s bookworms argue that the crowds, noise and flash photography are ruining the vibe of these once tranquil spaces.\n

data.title: India’s aim to diversify trade via Iran faces US tariff threat
data.description:
US President Donald Trump’s threat to impose 25 per cent tariffs on countries that do business with Iran has dented India’s ambition for the strategic Chabahar port and affected Delhi’s trade with Tehran.
Trump’s latest threat could curtail shipments of Indian basmati rice to Iran, but is likely to have a limited impact on India’s trade competitiveness since Iran accounts for about 0.3 per cent of its overall exports, according to analysts.
Shipments of the rice, one of India’s biggest exports to Iran, have slowed drastically amid nationwide protests across the Gulf country as Indian exporters hold back on new deals over concerns of non-payment. India, the world’s top rice supplier, has piled up record stocks of the grain after a bumper harvest last year.
The bigger impact for Delhi would be the Chabahar port in Iran’s southeastern Baluchistan province, which gives India a trade route to Afghanistan, central Asia and Europe, allowing it to bypass rival Pakistan.
India secured a six-month sanctions waiver from the US last October for the development and operation of the port. The waiver, which was extended until April this year, has enabled India’s Indian Ports Global Limited to continue managing the port under its 10-year agreement with Iran.
“Now if the period of extension expires and the US does not further extend the waiver, Indian companies will face the US penalty, and India’s long-term goal of regional connectivity will be in jeopardy, especially when India is rebuilding its relations with Afghanistan,” said Soumen Ray, a former Indian diplomat who has served in the Middle East.
The US has granted India the sanctions waiver for the development and operation of the Chabahar port on several occasions. Last September, the Trump administration revoked the waiver that had been in place since 2018, prompting Delhi to urge Washington to reverse its decision, which it did the following month.


The uncertainty over the port due to US pressure on Iran was a major concern for India as it aimed to diversify its exports and reduce reliance on its largest trade partner, said Biswajit Dhar, an economics professor at Delhi’s Council for Social Development.
“India’s ambitions for investing in the Chabahar port are mainly because it has been considering it as a gateway to Central Asia. It can become very difficult to access that market,” he added.
Srinivaasan Balakrishnan, director of strategic engagements and partnerships at Delhi-based think tank Indic Researchers, noted that India was actively lobbying Washington to extend the sanctions waiver on the port.
A failure to gain the waiver could slow or halt further investments in the port after various entities had committed US$500 million to the project, he said.
Washington has been piling pressure on Tehran amid violent crackdowns by Iranian security forces against protesters. As of Wednesday, the number of people killed in violence totalled more than 2,500, according to media reports.
Analysts warn that a US military action against Iran is imminent, as an American official said on Wednesday that Washington was withdrawing some personnel from bases in the Middle East.
The unstable situation in Iran meant that India would have to deal with “three verticals” simultaneously, Ray said.

“India has to maintain its strategic relationship with the US, its economic and regional interests with Iran and the growing use of trade as a geopolitical weapon by the US.”
India has so far refrained from making any public statement on the US tariff threat related to Iran. On Wednesday, Delhi strongly advised Indian nationals to avoid travel to Iran until further notice.
India’s exports to Iran totalled US$1.6 billion in 2024, mostly rice, tea, sugar, drugs and electrical machinery. In contrast, the goods and services that India exported to the US totalled US$129 billion that year.
New Delhi is already facing 50 per cent US tariffs, partly because it has purchased discounted Russian oil. The levy could rise further as Trump has backed a US bill to impose 500 per cent tariffs on countries that buy oil from Russia.
Meanwhile, India and the US are working on a trade deal under which Delhi is expected to address Washington’s demand to ease market access to several politically sensitive sectors in the South Asian country, such as agriculture.
Giving up on the export of goods to Iran would be a small sacrifice compared with India’s trade with the US, especially at a time when Indian businesses were wary of shipping goods to Iran due to the volatile situation in the Middle East, Dhar said.
“It is difficult to see how India will continue trade with Iran under the circumstances, as Trump’s wrath will be problematic,” he added.
-----
----

data.title: Taco Trump? It’s traders who are chickening out now
data.description:
One acronym has epitomised investors’ perception of US President Donald Trump’s resolve in following through on his threats to impose sky-high tariffs on America’s trading partners.
On May 2 last year, Financial Times commentator Robert Armstrong came up with “Taco”, which stands for “Trump always chickens out”. This was meant to describe the sharp rally in stock markets following Trump’s decision in April to suspend his “reciprocal” tariffs.
Further climbdowns, albeit partial ones, convinced many investors that Trump’s bark was worse than his bite. While other factors contributed to the strong gains in equity markets last year – the hype surrounding artificial intelligence being the most important one – Trump’s trade policies ceased to be the main determinant of sentiment by mid-2025.
In a report on May 14, Barclays went so far as to say “the US – and the world – seems to have turned the page on tariffs”. While the Taco narrative quickly took hold and reinforced markets’ belief that Trump’s presidency was transactional and non-ideological, it was deeply flawed.
For starters, the pressure on Trump from bond markets was negligible. The yield on the benchmark 10-year US Treasury bond fell from 4.7 per cent just before Trump’s second inauguration to 4 per cent in early September. It currently stands at 4.1 per cent.
The most important force constraining Trump on tariffs was China. Beijing’s decision to impose curbs on the export of rare earth elements – more than 85 per cent of which are produced in China and used in the manufacturing of everything from weapons systems to iPhones – revealed the strength of China’s leverage, forcing Trump to back down.


Even so, there was plenty of evidence that Trump was willing to inflict severe damage on US democracy and the economy. His evisceration of the rule of law, draconian measures to deport immigrants, cuts to the federal government workforce and, crucially, the unprecedented level of policy uncertainty undermined consumer and business confidence. The labour market, previously a source of resilience, came under more strain last year.
There was also sufficient proof that Trump was willing to risk causing a global recession by putting an end to predictable, rules-based trading relationships with the world’s largest economy. Furthermore, his decision in June to launch strikes on Iran’s key nuclear facilities showed he had a bigger appetite for military adventurism than supporters of isolationism in his Maga movement expected.
However, Trump’s latest assaults take his authoritarianism and recklessness to a whole new level. He has ordered the capture of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, flying him out of the country as part of a strategy of establishing US hegemony in the western hemisphere. He is also allowing the Justice Department to launch a criminal investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell over the US$2.5 billion renovation of the central bank’s headquarters.
Regime change in a country with the world’s largest proven oil reserves is a Rubicon-crossing moment in US foreign policy. This should serve as a sharp reality check for investors who underestimate and underprice geopolitical risk.


In its report on the top risks for 2026 earlier this month, Eurasia Group said the “Donroe doctrine” was meant to “actively assert American primacy through a mix of military pressure, economic coercion, selective alliance-building and Trump’s personal score-settling. In 2026, this posture will heighten the risk of policy overreach and unintended consequences.”
Iran appears to be the next foreign target of the Trump administration. This is despite the White House having no plan to deal with the aftermath of a change in regime and its struggles to convince US oil companies to invest in Venezuela given acute political and legal risks.
However, it is the weaponisation of the Justice Department against Powell that marks a more dangerous phase of Trump’s second term. While the criminal inquiry into the Fed chair has fanned fears about the independence of the world’s most influential central bank, the reasons for the attack run deeper.
Trump is trying to punish Powell for not lowering interest rates more sharply. That Powell himself deemed it necessary to call Trump out by pointing out that the threat of a criminal indictment was about whether “monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation” speaks volumes about the gravity of the threat to America’s institutions.


The attack on the Fed is counterproductive, mainly because it makes it more difficult for Trump’s nominee for Fed chair to be confirmed by the Senate. Moreover, it is hard to see how a new Trump-friendly chair could be seen as credible by other Fed policymakers. However, Trump does not care since “he is less interested in achieving policy results than he is punishing anyone who crosses him”, Nobel Prize-winning economist Paul Krugman wrote in his newsletter.
This is why investors’ lack of concern is so troubling. While global fund managers claim that inflation and a disorderly rise in bond yields are among the biggest risks in markets, US stocks are at record highs while Treasury yields have barely budged.
Investors are not putting their money where their mouth is. Complacency and the fear of missing out on further gains in asset prices are plausible explanations. A more troubling one is that Wall Street is afraid of Trump given his propensity for retribution.
Taco should stand for “traders always chicken out”. Markets, especially bond markets, are supposed to be a disciplining force. The longer investors give Trump a free pass, the more emboldened he will be to act recklessly at home and abroad.

data.title: Why US is splurging US$45 million on Thai-Cambodian border war amid cuts in aid funding
data.description:
Washington’s recent offer of US$45 million in funding to reinforce the Thailand-Cambodia ceasefire is a sign that the US is scrambling to regain the upper hand over China in mediating the rift between the Southeast Asian neighbours, according to analysts.
Announcing the funding on Friday, Michael DeSombre, assistant secretary of state for East Asian and Pacific affairs, said the Thai and Cambodian governments would strive to implement the Kuala Lumpur Peace Accords, signed last year when US President Donald Trump was in the Malaysian capital.
The gesture follows a renewed ceasefire on December 27 and trilateral talks with China in Yunnan province a day later, after a month of reignited tensions at the hotly disputed border that resulted in scores of deaths and millions displaced. Both countries have been clashing intermittently since tensions first flared in late July last year.
DeSombre said he was confident the ceasefire would hold, telling reporters that the US “is very focused on promoting a safe, secure, and prosperous Indo-Pacific, and all of our efforts globally are intended to help accomplish that goal”.

His statement came just ahead of his visits to top officials of both countries to ensure adherence to the ceasefire and discuss efforts for regional stability.
The amount from Washington, which shut down foreign aid arm USAID in the first few months of Trump’s administration, comprises US$15 million for border stabilisation, support for people displaced by the conflict and recovery aid for communities, US$10 million for demining and ordnance clearing operations, and US$20 million to combat scam operations and drug trafficking, and other programmes.
Sincere gesture or mere token?
Him Rotha, deputy director at the Cambodian Centre for Regional Studies, said the US wanted to emphasise its leading role in securing the ceasefire between Bangkok and Phnom Penh while tackling several issues at once.
Washington’s pledge was “more sophisticated” than the help provided by other countries, including China, as it sought to address issues beyond the border conflict, such as the dismantling of scam operations, he told This Week in Asia.
Sharon Seah, a senior fellow at the ISEAS – Yusof Ishak Institute, described the US move as indicative of its “uncanny ability to compartmentalise issues and problems”.
The Trump administration is facing a wave of criticisms at home, including for its strike on Venezuelan military sites, the fatal ICE Minnesota shooting, its withdrawal from several international organisations, and threats to take over Greenland.
Of note was the allocation for combating scam operations and drug trafficking, she said. In September, the US made tackling online fraud a priority after Americans lost an estimated US$10 billion from scam centres based in the region.


“US$20 million out of US$45 million is a fairly large portion going into this problem, which on the surface has nothing to do with the whole border conflict,” she said.
The offer appeared tokenistic – “not large enough to be that sincere, but not that small as to be insignificant” – she said.
There were also questions about its implementation. “In what ways would the US$15 million be spent on border stabilisation? Does it mean you will spend that money to build new homes, to bring people back home? This is a downstream issue right now,” she said. “The problem now is to stop the tit-for-tat reactions coming from both sides. You can’t even implement this package if there’s no peace.”
Deryk Baladjay, an international relations lecturer at De La Salle University, called the pledge a “significant opening gambit”.
“China recently pledged approximately US$2.8 million in emergency aid for the same issue. By committing nearly 16 times that amount, the US is making a loud statement about its intent to reclaim a leading role in the region.”
And allocating funds towards scam centres and narcotics showed the US was “addressing non-traditional security threats that directly impact both Southeast Asian stability and American economic interests”, he added.
The China spectre
China’s increasing influence in the peace efforts also prompted the offer, analysts say, as the US seeks to solidify its role as a peacemaker while maintaining its foothold in Southeast Asia.
Him said Washington’s pledge appeared strategic given China’s growing visibility and capacity to mend Thai-Cambodian ties.
It was designed for the US “to remain the partner of choice for Southeast Asian diplomacy”, Baladjay said. “By anchoring the ceasefire in a Western-funded framework of demining and border stabilisation, the US is ensuring that the peace process is not left exclusively to Chinese-mediated channels.”
The fund would also complement the Philippines’ Asean chairmanship this year, as Manila is Washington’s closest security and diplomatic ally in the region.

Baladjay cautioned, however, that such a strategy would succeed if it were closely coordinated with other Association of Southeast Asian Nations mechanisms, such as the newly deployed Asean Observer Teams.
“This gauged level of assistance signals that the US is ready to lead without overstepping, recognising that the Thai-Cambodian issue is a regional wound that must not be allowed to fester any longer,” he said.
Analysts also drew links between the package and the National Security Strategy released in November.
Washington’s interest in the region may be waning, as it turns towards the Americas to regain its supremacy in the western hemisphere, according to Seah.
“In that sense, what they’ve done with Venezuela is not a deviation. They have been thinking about how the US can regain its preeminent role in the western hemisphere and starting with Venezuela,” she said. “Southeast Asia is not mentioned at all except as a geographical point.”
However, Baladjay said it could be a sign that the Trump administration was seeking to swing the global narrative after backlash for its actions in Venezuela.
“It signals to the international community that the US can act as a constructive peace-builder in Asia, even while it maintains its role as a stern regulator in the western hemisphere,” he said.

data.title: South Korean leader Lee takes ‘pragmatic approach’ to reset ties with China, Japan
data.description:
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung’s back-to-back visits to China and Japan suggest a “pragmatic approach” to reset ties with both countries as tensions soar between Beijing and Tokyo, analysts said.
Lee met Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi on Tuesday during a two-day trip to Japan. Speaking to reporters after their summit in Nara – Takaichi’s hometown – Lee said there was “unprecedented turmoil” in the global political landscape and trade order.
He stressed the need for South Korea and Japan to expand cooperation at this “pivotal turning point in civilisation”.
Lee said the two leaders shared a “common understanding” of the importance of cooperation between South Korea, Japan and the United States for regional peace and stability.
He said there was a need for South Korea and Japan to also work with China, and for the three nations “to identify as much common ground as possible to communicate and cooperate together”.
But Takaichi did not mention China in her remarks, instead stressing the need for trilateral cooperation between Japan, South Korea and the US.
Lee’s summit with Takaichi came only a week after he made a state visit to China for talks with President Xi Jinping in Beijing last Monday.

Tensions have been running high between Beijing and Tokyo over the suggestion by Takaichi in November that Japan could take military action if the People’s Liberation Army attacked Taiwan.
Her remarks prompted strong protests from Beijing, as well as economic and diplomatic retaliation.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited, by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US and its treaty allies South Korea and Japan, do not recognise self-governed Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any forcible change to the status quo and is legally bound to supply Taipei with weapons for defence.
Seoul has taken a neutral stance as the rift between China and Japan has deepened, wary that it could affect South Korea’s own security and economic interests.
Ahead of his trip to Beijing, Lee told state broadcaster CCTV that Seoul “respects” the one-China policy and stressed the importance of “maintaining peace and stability in Northeast Asia”.
During their talks in the Chinese capital last week, Lee said 2026 was the year for the “full restoration of South Korea-China relations”, while Xi said both countries should “stand firmly on the right side of history and make correct strategic choices”.
Those remarks follow nearly a decade of strained ties between the two nations after Beijing imposed unofficial sanctions on Seoul when it allowed Washington to deploy a THAAD – Terminal High-Altitude Area Defence – missile defence system in South Korea in 2016.


Choi Eun-mi, a research fellow at the Asan Institute for Policy Studies in Seoul, noted the “subtle contrast” in Lee and Takaichi’s remarks, with Lee emphasising Northeast Asian cooperation and Takaichi focused on ties between Tokyo, Seoul and Washington.
“[Lee’s] statement, made amid escalating tensions between China and Japan, is seen as a pragmatic statement of intent for regional peace and stability,” Choi said.
Troy Stangarone, a non-resident fellow at the US-based Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology, said Lee was looking to reset relations with both countries.
He said the “shift” with Japan began under the previous administration and Lee wanted to build on those improved ties while resetting relations with China.
“The proximity of the two summits is designed to emphasise the importance the Lee administration places on relations with both countries,” he said.
And for Beijing, the main interest was preventing a “convergence” of views on Taiwan’s future between Seoul and Tokyo, according to Stangarone.
“Lee and Takaichi were careful not to place cooperation between Seoul and Tokyo in the context of the dispute between China and Japan,” he added.
As Seoul’s relations with its two neighbours improve, the feud between Beijing and Tokyo is deepening. China last week imposed a ban on the export of dual-use goods – with both civilian and military applications – to Japan.
The list includes rare earth metals and minerals that are used in everything from defence to electric vehicles, energy and electronics. China currently dominates the global supply of rare earths and other critical minerals, accounting for more than 90 per cent of it.
In Nara, Takaichi said the two leaders had pledged to promote “strategic and mutually beneficial cooperation” in economic security, including supply chains.
Stangarone said China’s use of critical minerals as a tool of “coercive” foreign policy was driving other nations like Japan to find alternative sources.
“These moves are focused on the long term rather than immediately addressing any negative impacts from China’s new restrictions. The United States, South Korea and Japan are already engaged in trilateral cooperation to diversify their supply of critical minerals,” he said.
“Deeper bilateral cooperation on this issue would benefit both Seoul and Tokyo, but it will take years to develop meaningful alternatives. Japan, for example, has been working to diversify its supply of critical minerals from China since 2010. Despite progress over time, Tokyo has seen its dependence on China deepen in recent years.”
Stangarone said Lee’s push for cooperation among South Korea, Japan and China was an extension of his “pragmatic approach” to foreign policy. He said their “interconnected” supply chains meant Chinese export restrictions on Japan would also indirectly affect the South Korean economy.
“China’s recent ban on exporting dual-use items to Japan impacts South Korea’s semiconductor, battery, display and other industries,” he said.
He said tensions between China and Japan also had a negative impact on efforts to deal with North Korea’s nuclear programme.
“Lee is trying to encourage stability and cooperation among the region’s three main players’ relations to address regional security challenges and maintain economic growth,” Stangarone said.


Choi said while Takaichi had previously called for supply chains to be strengthened in talks between Tokyo and Seoul they were “particularly significant” now, given the mounting pressure from China’s restrictions on dual-use goods.
“This is expected to lead to stronger cooperation between South Korea and Japan in substantive areas such as AI, semiconductors and advanced industries,” Choi said. “This can be seen as a shift towards cooperation in economic security and future industries.”

data.title: Hong Kong authorities halt use of elevating platform at work site after worker death
data.description:
Hong Kong labour authorities have suspended the use of elevating platforms at a construction site where a 65-year-old worker died after getting trapped between the device’s railing and a wall.
The Labour Department on Thursday said it was “saddened” by the death and had launched an investigation to identify the cause of the accident that took place at the Victoria Blossom housing project on Shing Fung Lane in Kai Tak on Wednesday afternoon.
A spokesman said the department had issued suspension notices to the contractors to halt the use of elevating work platforms on the site, saying measures to improve safety were needed.
“The contractors cannot resume the work process until the Labour Department is satisfied that suitable measures to abate the relevant risks have been taken,” he said.
The accident occurred as the worker was using the power-operated elevating work platform for plastering work. He was found unconscious at 4.20pm and died half an hour later at United Christian Hospital in Kwun Tong.
A colleague, 63, also sustained head injuries and was taken to hospital.
The project by developer CK Asset Holdings is located at the former airport runway and will be developed in three phases, with 1,005 homes in total.
The department spokesman added that the investigation would be completed in a timely manner to assess the liability of those in charge of the project and recommend improvement measures.
He said the department did not rule out the possibility of prosecuting liable parties who violated work safety rules.
The Post has reached out to CK Asset Holdings to inquire about the company’s follow-up action after the accident.
Separately, the department also issued a suspension notice to the contractor overseeing a site on Po Fung Road in Tsuen Wan, where a worker was killed and two others injured on Wednesday after they were hit by gas cylinders that fell out of a metal cage hoisted by a tower crane.
The contractor was told to stop lifting operations at the site, with the department saying such work could only resume after adequate safety measures were put in place, as investigations continued.

data.title: Singapore’s PM Lawrence Wong removes Pritam Singh as Leader of the Opposition
data.description:
Singapore’s Prime Minister Lawrence Wong has stripped Workers’ Party chief Pritam Singh of his role as the city state’s first Leader of the Opposition following Singh’s conviction for lying under oath to a parliamentary committee.
Singh assumed the official role in 2020 following the general election where his party secured a record 10 elected seats in parliament. Then prime minister Lee Hsien Loong announced during a press conference that Singh will be provided with staff and resources.
Following the 2025 general election, Lee’s successor Lawrence Wong reinstated the title to Singh.
However, on Thursday, Wong stripped Singh of the title and privileges a day after parliament voted that he was unsuitable to continue as the leader of the opposition following his conviction.
“Having considered the matter carefully, I have decided that Mr Singh’s criminal convictions, taken together with Parliament’s considered view of his unsuitability, make it no longer tenable for him to continue as the LO,” Wong said in a statement.
Singh, MP for Aljunied constituency, was fined S$14,000 (US$10,880) for lying under oath to a parliamentary committee looking into the lie former WP MP Raeesah Khan told in parliament. His conviction was upheld on December 4 after he appealed against it.
